1:
"Moreover, brethren, we do you to wit of the grace of God bestowed on the churches of Macedonia;" The witnesses note the grace of God shown in the Macedonian churches' giving. On "we do you to wit (make known to you) of the grace of God," the grammatical voices note the announcement. Barnes and Robertson note Paul introduces the great theme of chapters 8–9, the collection for the poor saints, by making known "the grace of God bestowed on the churches of Macedonia" — the liberality of the Macedonian believers being itself a gift of God's grace.

On "the grace of God," the witnesses note the source. Barnes and Calvin note Paul calls their generosity "the grace of God" — not merely human kindness but a work of God's grace in them, that they should give so freely; the giving spirit is God's gift.

The puritan Henry and Reformed Calvin note the design: Paul holds up the Macedonians' example to stir the Corinthians to the same liberality. The verse displays the grace of God shown in the Macedonian churches' giving.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul introduces the collection by making known "the grace of God bestowed on the churches of Macedonia" — their liberality itself a gift of God's grace (Barnes, Robertson); calling their generosity "the grace of God," not mere human kindness but a work of grace in them (Barnes, Calvin). Henry and Calvin note Paul holds up their example to stir the Corinthians. The verse displays the grace in the Macedonians. The verse marks the announcement: the grace of God. The verse displays the giving: of the Macedonians. The verse displays the source: God's grace. The verse displays the design: to stir the Corinthians. The grace displays the Macedonians' liberality as God's gift. The verse displays the making known: the grace. The verse displays the churches: of Macedonia. The verse displays the example: held up. The grace of God shown in the Macedonian churches' giving displays the divine source of true liberality: Paul makes known their generosity as itself a gift of God's grace, holding up their example to move the Corinthians to the same. The verse displays the grace of giving, illustrated by the Macedonians' joyful poverty (v.2).
2:
"How that in a great trial of affliction the abundance of their joy and their deep poverty abounded unto the riches of their liberality." The witnesses note the Macedonians' liberality out of affliction and deep poverty. On "in a great trial of affliction," the grammatical voices note the circumstances. Barnes and Robertson note the Macedonians gave amid "a great trial of affliction" — severe persecution and hardship; their generosity came not from ease but from the midst of suffering.

On "the abundance of their joy and their deep poverty abounded unto the riches of their liberality," the witnesses note the paradox. Barnes and Calvin note the striking combination: "the abundance of their joy" (spiritual gladness) and their "deep poverty" (extreme material want) together "abounded unto the riches of their liberality" — their joy and even their poverty overflowed in rich generosity.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the wonder: that the deeply poor, in affliction, should give richly and joyfully is the marvel of grace; their want did not hinder but heightened their bounty. The verse displays the Macedonians' liberality out of affliction and poverty.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Macedonians gave amid "a great trial of affliction" (severe persecution), not from ease but from suffering (Barnes, Robertson); and the striking paradox — "the abundance of their joy" and their "deep poverty" together "abounded unto the riches of their liberality," their joy and even their poverty overflowing in rich generosity (Barnes, Calvin). Wesley notes the marvel that the deeply poor should give richly and joyfully. The verse displays liberality from poverty. The verse marks the affliction: great trial. The verse displays the paradox: joy and poverty. The verse displays the overflow: riches of liberality. The verse displays the marvel: of grace. The liberality displays joy and poverty overflowing in generosity. The verse displays the affliction: severe. The verse displays the poverty: deep. The verse displays the giving: rich. The Macedonians' liberality out of affliction and deep poverty displays the marvel of grace in giving: amid severe persecution and extreme want, their overflowing joy and even their poverty abounded in rich generosity — proof that true liberality springs not from abundance but from grace. The verse displays the wonder of joyful giving, pressed beyond their power (vv.3-5).
3:
"For to their power, I bear record, yea, and beyond their power they were willing of themselves;" The brief verse notes the Macedonians giving beyond their power, of their own accord. The grammatical voices note the measure. Barnes and Robertson note Paul testifies ("I bear record") that the Macedonians gave "to their power" (up to the full extent of their means) "and beyond their power" — even more than could reasonably be expected of their poverty.

On "they were willing of themselves," the witnesses note the spontaneity. Barnes and Calvin note that they gave "of themselves" (of their own accord, freely, spontaneously) — not under pressure or persuasion, but from their own willing hearts; the giving was voluntary.

The Reformed Calvin notes the example: the Macedonians needed no urging but pressed forward of themselves, giving beyond their means — a rebuke to grudging or reluctant giving. The verse displays the Macedonians giving beyond their power, of their own accord.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul testifies the Macedonians gave "to their power" (the full extent of their means) "and beyond their power," more than could be expected of their poverty (Barnes, Robertson); and "of themselves" (of their own accord, freely, spontaneously), not under pressure but from willing hearts (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes they needed no urging but pressed forward of themselves. The verse displays giving beyond their power. The verse marks the measure: to and beyond their power. The verse displays the spontaneity: of themselves. The verse displays the freedom: voluntary. The verse displays the example: needing no urging. The giving displays the Macedonians' free, abundant generosity. The verse displays the power: exceeded. The verse displays the willingness: their own. The verse displays the freedom: spontaneous. The Macedonians giving beyond their power, of their own accord, displays the freeness of true liberality: they gave to the full of their means and beyond, not pressed or persuaded but freely and spontaneously of their own willing hearts — a model of voluntary generosity. The verse displays the spontaneity of grace-filled giving, even begging to share (v.4).
4:
"Praying us with much intreaty that we would receive the gift, and take upon us the fellowship of the ministering to the saints." The brief verse notes the Macedonians begging to share in the ministry to the saints. The grammatical voices note the eagerness. Barnes and Robertson note the remarkable detail: the Macedonians, far from needing to be asked, "prayed us with much intreaty" (earnestly begged Paul) that he "would receive the gift" — they pleaded for the privilege of giving.

On "the fellowship of the ministering to the saints," the witnesses note the privilege. Barnes and Calvin note they sought "the fellowship (koinonia, participation, partnership) of the ministering to the saints" — they counted it an honour and a grace to have a share in relieving the poor believers; giving was a privilege they begged to enjoy.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the lesson: the Macedonians regarded the opportunity to give as a favour, not a burden; they entreated for the chance to help. The verse displays the Macedonians begging to share in the ministry to the saints.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Macedonians, far from needing to be asked, "prayed us with much intreaty" that Paul "would receive the gift," pleading for the privilege of giving (Barnes, Robertson); they sought "the fellowship of the ministering to the saints," counting it an honour and grace to share in relieving the poor (Barnes, Calvin). Wesley notes they regarded the chance to give as a favour, not a burden. The verse displays begging to share. The verse marks the intreaty: much begging. The verse displays the request: receive the gift. The verse displays the privilege: fellowship of ministering. The verse displays the lesson: giving a favour. The begging displays the Macedonians counting giving a privilege. The verse displays the entreaty: earnest. The verse displays the gift: pressed. The verse displays the fellowship: sought. The Macedonians begging to share in the ministry to the saints displays the height of their grace: they earnestly entreated Paul to receive their gift and counted it an honour to share in relieving the poor believers — regarding the opportunity to give as a privilege to be begged for, not a burden. The verse displays the eagerness of grace-filled giving, springing from self-surrender (v.5).
5:
"And this they did, not as we hoped, but first gave their own selves to the Lord, and unto us by the will of God." The brief verse notes the Macedonians first giving themselves to the Lord. The grammatical voices note the surpassing of expectation. Barnes and Robertson note the Macedonians acted "not as we hoped" — they exceeded Paul's expectations — for "first" they "gave their own selves to the Lord," a self-dedication deeper than the gift of money.

On "first gave their own selves to the Lord, and unto us," the witnesses note the order. Barnes and Calvin note the right order: they gave themselves first to the Lord (in entire consecration) and then "unto us" (to Paul, as his fellow-workers in the cause) "by the will of God" — the gift of substance flowed from the prior gift of self.

The Reformed Calvin notes the principle: the giving of self to God is the root of all true liberality; once the heart is wholly the Lord's, the offering of money follows freely. The verse displays the Macedonians first giving themselves to the Lord.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Macedonians acted "not as we hoped," exceeding Paul's expectations, for "first" they "gave their own selves to the Lord," a self-dedication deeper than money (Barnes, Robertson); the right order — themselves first to the Lord, then "unto us" (his fellow-workers) "by the will of God," the gift of substance flowing from the gift of self (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes the giving of self is the root of all true liberality. The verse displays giving themselves first. The verse marks the surpassing: not as we hoped. The verse displays the order: themselves first. The verse displays the dedication: to the Lord. The verse displays the principle: self before substance. The giving displays self-dedication as the root of liberality. The verse displays the selves: given first. The verse displays the Lord: their portion. The verse displays the will: of God. The Macedonians first giving themselves to the Lord displays the root of their liberality: exceeding all expectation, they gave not money first but their own selves to the Lord, and then to his servants — the offering of substance flowing from the prior, deeper gift of self. The verse displays self-surrender as the spring of giving, prompting Paul to urge the same on Corinth (v.6).
6:
"Insomuch that we desired Titus, that as he had begun, so he would also finish in you the same grace also." The brief verse notes Titus sent to complete the collection at Corinth. The grammatical voices note the commission. Barnes and Robertson note that, moved by the Macedonian example, Paul "desired (urged) Titus" — who had earlier "begun" the collection among the Corinthians — to return and "finish (complete) in you the same grace also."

On "the same grace," the witnesses note the term. Barnes and Calvin note Paul calls the collection "this grace" — the gracious work of giving — and wished Titus to bring it to completion among the Corinthians, as he had begun it.

The grammatical-historical note the continuity: the Corinthians had made an earlier start (a year before, v.10); Paul now sends Titus to see the work brought to a worthy finish. The verse displays Titus sent to complete the collection at Corinth.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: moved by the Macedonian example, Paul "desired (urged) Titus" — who had "begun" the collection among the Corinthians — to "finish in you the same grace also" (Barnes, Robertson); calling the collection "this grace," the gracious work of giving, to be brought to completion (Barnes, Calvin). The grammatical-historical note the Corinthians' earlier start, now to be finished. The verse displays Titus sent to finish. The verse marks the commission: Titus desired. The verse displays the work: begun and to finish. The verse displays the term: the same grace. The verse displays the continuity: an earlier start. The commission displays Titus sent to complete the gracious work. The verse displays the desiring: of Titus. The verse displays the finishing: of the collection. The verse displays the grace: of giving. Titus sent to complete the collection at Corinth displays Paul's practical step: stirred by the Macedonians, he urged Titus, who had begun the collection among the Corinthians, to return and bring the same gracious work to completion. The verse displays the renewal of the collection, set among the Corinthians' other graces (v.7).
7:
"Therefore, as ye abound in every thing, in faith, and utterance, and knowledge, and in all diligence, and in your love to us, see that ye abound in this grace also." The witnesses note the call to abound in the grace of giving as in other graces. On "as ye abound in every thing," the grammatical voices note the Corinthians' gifts. Barnes and Robertson note Paul lists the graces in which the Corinthians excelled: "faith," "utterance" (speech, eloquence), "knowledge," "all diligence" (earnestness), and "your love to us" — a richly gifted church.

On "see that ye abound in this grace also," the witnesses note the appeal. Barnes and Calvin note the exhortation: since they abounded in so many graces, let them "abound in this grace also" — the grace of liberality; their giving should match their other excellences.

The eastern Chrysostom and puritan Henry note the point: liberality is a grace to be cultivated like faith, knowledge, and love; a church rich in gifts should not be poor in generosity. The verse displays the call to abound in the grace of giving.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul lists the graces in which the Corinthians excelled — "faith," "utterance" (speech), "knowledge," "all diligence," and "your love to us," a richly gifted church (Barnes, Robertson); and exhorts that, abounding in so many graces, they "abound in this grace also," the grace of liberality matching their other excellences (Barnes, Calvin). Chrysostom and Henry note liberality is a grace to be cultivated like the rest. The verse displays abounding in giving. The verse marks the gifts: faith, knowledge, love. The verse displays the appeal: abound in this grace. The verse displays the matching: giving to other graces. The verse displays the lesson: liberality a grace. The call displays giving cultivated as the other graces. The verse displays the abounding: in all. The verse displays the grace: of giving. The verse displays the appeal: to match. The call to abound in the grace of giving as in other graces displays Paul's gentle appeal: since the Corinthians excelled in faith, utterance, knowledge, diligence, and love, they should likewise abound in liberality — generosity being a grace to be cultivated alongside the rest. The verse displays giving as a grace, urged not by command but by love's proof (v.8).
8:
"I speak not by commandment, but by occasion of the forwardness of others, and to prove the sincerity of your love." The brief verse notes Paul urging giving not by command but to test their love. The grammatical voices note the manner. Barnes and Robertson note Paul carefully says "I speak not by commandment" — he does not lay it on them as an authoritative order; giving must be voluntary, not coerced.

On "by occasion of the forwardness of others," the witnesses note the example. Barnes and Calvin note Paul appeals instead to "the forwardness (eagerness, zeal) of others" — the Macedonians' example — to stir the Corinthians by comparison, not compulsion.

On "to prove the sincerity of your love," the witnesses note the aim. Barnes notes Paul's purpose: to "prove (test) the sincerity (genuineness) of your love" — for liberality is a true test of love; willing generosity shows love to be real. The verse displays Paul urging giving not by command but to test their love.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul says "I speak not by commandment," not laying it on them as an order, for giving must be voluntary (Barnes, Robertson); he appeals instead to "the forwardness of others" (the Macedonians' eagerness), stirring them by example not compulsion (Barnes, Calvin); his aim "to prove the sincerity of your love," liberality being a true test of love (Barnes). The verse displays urging not by command. The verse marks the manner: not by commandment. The verse displays the example: forwardness of others. The verse displays the aim: to prove their love. The verse displays the test: of sincerity. The urging displays giving as a voluntary test of love. The verse displays the freedom: not commanded. The verse displays the example: of others. The verse displays the love: tested. Paul urging giving not by command but to test their love displays the spirit of his appeal: he lays no order upon them, but, pointing to the eagerness of others, would prove the sincerity of their love — for willing liberality is the true test of a genuine heart. The verse displays love proved by giving, grounded in the supreme example of Christ (v.9).
9:
"For ye know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, that, though he was rich, yet for your sakes he became poor, that ye through his poverty might be rich." The witnesses note the supreme example of Christ's self-impoverishing grace. On "though he was rich, yet for your sakes he became poor," the great point divides over Christ's riches. The grammatical-historical Barnes, Ellicott, and the Reformed Calvin hold that Christ "was rich" in his pre-existent divine glory and as Lord and Maker of all things, and "became poor" by taking human nature in the incarnation (emptying himself, Philippians 2:6-7) and choosing a life and death of poverty. Wesleyan-arminian Clarke joins them and presses the argument against the Socinians: if Christ were a mere man, in what sense was he rich? — his pre-existent riches and deity are necessary to the sense.

On "that ye through his poverty might be rich," the witnesses note the purpose. Barnes and Cyril note Christ's purpose: that "ye through his poverty might be rich" — rich in the blessings of the gospel, the favour of God, and eternal glory; his self-impoverishing the means of our enrichment.

The witnesses note the argument: this supreme example of self-giving love is the great motive to liberality. The verse displays the supreme example of Christ's self-impoverishing grace.

The witnesses agree the purpose and force: Christ "became poor that ye through his poverty might be rich" — rich in the gospel's blessings, God's favour, and eternal glory — this supreme self-giving love being the great motive to liberality (Barnes, Calvin, Cyril). But "though he was rich" carries a doctrinal weight: Barnes, Ellicott, Calvin, and Clarke take it of Christ's pre-existent divine glory and lordship over all, "becoming poor" by the incarnation (Philippians 2:6-7) and a life of poverty — Barnes and Clarke explicitly refuting the Socinian reading ("rich in power and the Spirit," denying pre-existence) as failing the sense, since a mere man could not be called rich nor enrich a multitude by his poverty. The verse displays Christ's self-impoverishing grace. The verse marks the riches: pre-existent glory. The verse displays the poverty: the incarnation. The verse displays the purpose: that we be rich. The verse displays the debate: pre-existence vs Socinian. The example displays Christ's self-emptying the motive to give. The verse displays the rich: Christ. The verse displays the poor: he became. The verse displays the riches: ours through him. The supreme example of Christ's self-impoverishing grace displays the great motive to liberality: he who was rich in divine glory became poor in the incarnation for our sakes, that through his poverty we might be made eternally rich — his self-giving (against the Socinian denial of his pre-existent riches) the pattern for ours. The verse displays the grace of Christ as the spring of Christian giving, applied in practical counsel (vv.10-12).
10:
"And herein I give my advice: for this is expedient for you, who have begun before, not only to do, but also to be forward a year ago." The brief verse notes Paul's advice that the Corinthians complete what they began a year before. The grammatical voices note the counsel. Barnes and Robertson note Paul "gives advice" (his judgment, not a command) that completing the collection "is expedient (profitable, fitting) for you" — for their own good and credit.

On "who have begun before... a year ago," the witnesses note the prior start. Barnes and Henry note the Corinthians had "begun before" — a year ago they had been "forward" (eager) both "to do" and "to be willing"; they had made an early and willing start, ahead even of the Macedonians.

The Reformed Calvin notes the gentle pressure: having begun so promptly a year before, it would be to their shame not to finish; Paul reminds them of their own earlier readiness. The verse displays Paul's advice that the Corinthians complete what they began.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul "gives advice" (his judgment, not a command) that completing the collection "is expedient (fitting) for you," for their own good (Barnes, Robertson); the Corinthians had "begun before" — a year ago they were "forward" both to do and to will, an early willing start (Barnes, Henry). Calvin notes that having begun so promptly, it would be to their shame not to finish. The verse displays completing what they began. The verse marks the advice: my judgment. The verse displays the expediency: for their good. The verse displays the start: a year ago. The verse displays the pressure: to finish. The advice displays the Corinthians urged to finish their early start. The verse displays the counsel: given. The verse displays the beginning: a year before. The verse displays the completion: due. Paul's advice that the Corinthians complete what they began a year before displays his gentle counsel: it is expedient for them, who had made so early and willing a start, now to bring the work to completion — lest their prompt beginning be left unfinished. The verse displays the call to finish the collection, matched to readiness of will (v.11).
11:
"Now therefore perform the doing of it; that as there was a readiness to will, so there may be a performance also out of that which ye have." The brief verse notes the call to match willing with performance. The grammatical voices note the exhortation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul urges them now to "perform the doing of it" (complete the act) — that the work begun be actually finished, not left at good intentions.

On "as there was a readiness to will, so there may be a performance," the witnesses note the matching. Barnes and Calvin note the principle: as there had been "a readiness to will" (a prompt willingness), so let there be "a performance" (the actual doing) — willing must issue in deed.

On "out of that which ye have," the witnesses note the measure: the performance is to be "out of that which ye have" — according to their means, not beyond. The verse displays the call to match willing with performance.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul urges them to "perform the doing of it," that the work begun be finished, not left at good intentions (Barnes, Robertson); the principle — as there was "a readiness to will," so let there be "a performance," willing issuing in deed (Barnes, Calvin); and this "out of that which ye have," according to their means (Barnes). The verse displays matching willing with doing. The verse marks the call: perform it. The verse displays the principle: will issuing in deed. The verse displays the measure: out of what you have. The verse displays the completion: of the work. The call displays willing brought to actual performance. The verse displays the doing: completed. The verse displays the readiness: of will. The verse displays the means: their own. The call to match willing with performance displays Paul's practical urging: the prompt willingness of a year ago must now issue in the actual doing, completed out of what they have — that good intention be crowned with deed. The verse displays the joining of will and work, accepted according to ability (v.12).
12:
"For if there be first a willing mind, it is accepted according to that a man hath, and not according to that he hath not." The witnesses note that the gift is accepted according to what one has, by the willing mind. On "if there be first a willing mind," the grammatical voices note the first thing. Barnes and Robertson note Paul lays down the governing principle: "if there be first a willing mind (a ready, cheerful disposition)" — the willing heart is the first and essential consideration; without it no gift is acceptable, and with it any gift is.

On "accepted according to that a man hath, not according to that he hath not," the witnesses note the measure. Barnes and Wesley note that the gift "is accepted according to that a man hath" — God measures the offering by the giver's means and willingness, not by an amount beyond his ability; God does not require what one has not.

The grammatical-historical note the comfort (with the widow's mite, Mark 12:42-44): the poor man's small gift, given willingly, is as acceptable as the rich man's large one; obligation is proportioned to ability. The verse displays the gift accepted according to what one has.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul lays down the principle — "if there be first a willing mind" (a ready, cheerful disposition), the willing heart being the first and essential thing, without which no gift is acceptable and with which any is (Barnes, Robertson); the gift "accepted according to that a man hath," God measuring it by the giver's means and willingness, not requiring what one has not (Barnes, Wesley); the poor man's small gift, given willingly, as acceptable as the rich man's (the widow's mite, Mark 12:42-44). The verse displays the gift accepted by the willing mind. The verse marks the first thing: a willing mind. The verse displays the measure: what one has. The verse displays the limit: not what one has not. The verse displays the comfort: the widow's mite. The principle displays the willing heart making any gift acceptable. The verse displays the willingness: essential. The verse displays the means: the measure. The verse displays the acceptance: of the willing. That the gift is accepted according to what one has, by the willing mind, displays the gracious principle of giving: God looks first to the ready heart, and measures the offering by the giver's means and willingness, not by what he does not have — so the poor man's willing mite is as acceptable as the rich man's abundance. The verse displays the equity of God's acceptance, applied to mutual relief (vv.13-15).
13:
"For I mean not that other men be eased, and ye burdened:" The brief verse notes that Paul does not intend others eased and the Corinthians burdened. The grammatical voices note the disclaimer. Barnes and Robertson note Paul guards against misunderstanding: "I mean not that other men be eased (relieved) and ye burdened (distressed)" — he does not wish the Corinthians impoverished in order that others may live at ease.

On the balance, the witnesses note the fairness. Barnes and Calvin note Paul's concern for equity: the burden of charity should be fairly shared, not laid so heavily on the Corinthians that they suffer want while others are comfortable.

The Reformed Calvin notes the guard (against the fanatics who demand community of goods): God does not require us to reduce ourselves to straits that others may be at ease; the rule is proportionate relief, not the abolition of all distinction. The verse displays that Paul does not intend others eased and the Corinthians burdened.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul guards against misunderstanding — "I mean not that other men be eased and ye burdened," not wishing the Corinthians impoverished that others live at ease (Barnes, Robertson); his concern for equity, the burden fairly shared, not laid so heavily that they suffer want (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes the guard against the fanatics who demand community of goods — God requires proportionate relief, not self-impoverishment. The verse displays no burdening the Corinthians. The verse marks the disclaimer: not eased and burdened. The verse displays the equity: fairly shared. The verse displays the guard: against community of goods. The verse displays the rule: proportionate relief. The disclaimer displays fair, proportionate giving, not self-impoverishment. The verse displays the easing: of others. The verse displays the burden: not on you. The verse displays the fairness: intended. That Paul does not intend others eased and the Corinthians burdened displays the equity of his appeal: he does not wish them impoverished that others may be at ease, but seeks a fair sharing of the burden — proportionate relief, not (against the fanatics) the reducing of givers to want. The verse displays the principle of equity, defined as mutual supply (v.14).
14:
"But by an equality, that now at this time your abundance may be a supply for their want, that their abundance also may be a supply for your want: that there may be equality:" The witnesses note the principle of equality — mutual supply among the churches. On "by an equality," the grammatical voices note the principle. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's rule is "equality" (a fair proportion, a balancing) — that now the Corinthians' "abundance" should supply the Jerusalem saints' "want," and that, should circumstances reverse, the others' abundance might one day supply the Corinthians' want.

On the meaning of "equality," interpreters guard against extremes. The Reformed Calvin understands it as the communion of saints — a befitting proportion in which members mutually supply each other, not an absolute leveling of all property (he opposes the "fanatics" who would make all things common). Grammatical-historical Ellicott rebuts the Roman Catholic reading that "equality" means a transfer of superfluous merits, calling it reading too much between the lines.

The witnesses note the design: a mutual, proportionate sharing whereby no believer is left in want while another has abundance. The verse displays the principle of equality — mutual supply among the churches.

The witnesses agree the principle: "equality" is a fair, proportionate balancing whereby the Corinthians' present abundance supplies the Jerusalem saints' want, and (should circumstances reverse) the others' abundance might one day supply theirs — a mutual relief so that none is left in want while another abounds (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin). But its meaning is guarded against extremes: Calvin understands it as the communion of saints' proportionate mutual supply, not an absolute leveling of property (against the fanatics who would make all common); Ellicott rebuts the Roman Catholic reading that "equality" means a transfer of superfluous merits. The verse displays equality as mutual supply. The verse marks the principle: equality. The verse displays the supply: abundance to want. The verse displays the mutuality: each to each. The verse displays the guards: not communism, not merit-transfer. The principle displays proportionate mutual relief among the churches. The verse displays the abundance: shared. The verse displays the want: supplied. The verse displays the equality: a balance. The principle of equality — mutual supply among the churches — displays the rule of Christian charity: a fair, proportionate balancing in which present abundance relieves present want, and might one day be relieved in turn — neither a forced community of goods (Calvin's guard) nor a transfer of merits (Ellicott's), but the communion of saints supplying each other's needs. The verse displays the equity of mutual relief, illustrated by the manna (v.15).
15:
"As it is written, He that had gathered much had nothing over; and he that had gathered little had no lack." The brief verse notes the manna as the pattern of equality in supply. The grammatical voices note the citation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul quotes Exodus 16:18 (the gathering of the manna): "he that gathered much had nothing over, and he that gathered little had no lack" — when measured, all had enough and none too much.

On the application, the witnesses note the pattern. Barnes and Calvin note Paul applies the manna as an emblem: as God so ordered the manna that, after sharing, all were supplied and none had a surplus, so among Christians the abundance of some should supply the want of others, that all may have enough.

The Reformed Calvin notes the warning: as hoarded manna bred worms and stank, so wealth heaped up at the expense of needy brethren is accursed; God gives abundance not for excess but for relieving want. The verse displays the manna as the pattern of equality in supply.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul quotes Exodus 16:18 (the manna) — "he that gathered much had nothing over, and he that gathered little had no lack," all having enough when measured (Barnes, Robertson); applying it as an emblem — as God ordered the manna so that all were supplied and none had surplus, so the abundance of some should supply the want of others (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes the warning that hoarded wealth, like hoarded manna, is accursed. The verse displays the manna pattern. The verse marks the citation: the manna. The verse displays the supply: all had enough. The verse displays the application: abundance supplies want. The verse displays the warning: against hoarding. The pattern displays the manna as the emblem of equal supply. The verse displays the much: nothing over. The verse displays the little: no lack. The verse displays the equality: of supply. The manna as the pattern of equality in supply displays the scriptural emblem of Christian charity: as God so ordered the manna that, after sharing, the one who gathered much had no surplus and the one who gathered little no lack, so the abundance of some is meant to supply the want of others — that all may have enough and none hoard. The verse displays the divine pattern of sufficiency, before Paul commends the messengers (vv.16-24).
16:
"But thanks be to God, which put the same earnest care into the heart of Titus for you." The brief verse notes Paul's thanks for Titus's God-given care for the Corinthians. The grammatical voices note the thanksgiving. Barnes and Robertson note Paul gives "thanks to God" who "put the same earnest care (diligence, zeal) into the heart of Titus for you" — Titus's concern for the Corinthians is ascribed to God as its author.

On "the same earnest care," the witnesses note the source. Barnes and Chrysostom note that Titus shared Paul's own earnest care for the Corinthians, and this is traced to God, who put it in his heart; the zeal of faithful ministers is God's gift.

The Reformed Calvin notes the point: every good disposition in Christ's servants, including Titus's care for the church, comes from God, and so calls for thanksgiving to him. The verse displays Paul's thanks for Titus's God-given care.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul gives "thanks to God" who "put the same earnest care into the heart of Titus for you," Titus's concern ascribed to God as its author (Barnes, Robertson); Titus sharing Paul's own earnest care, traced to God who put it in his heart (Barnes, Chrysostom). Calvin notes every good disposition in Christ's servants comes from God and calls for thanksgiving. The verse displays thanks for Titus's care. The verse marks the thanks: to God. The verse displays the care: put in Titus. The verse displays the source: God the author. The verse displays the zeal: a gift. The thanksgiving displays Titus's care as God's gift. The verse displays the thanks: given. The verse displays the care: earnest. The verse displays the heart: of Titus. Paul's thanks for Titus's God-given care for the Corinthians displays the divine source of ministerial zeal: the same earnest care that Paul felt, God had put into the heart of Titus — and so Paul gives thanks to him, the author of every good disposition in his servants. The verse displays the God-given concern of Titus, who went willingly (v.17).
17:
"For indeed he accepted the exhortation; but being more forward, of his own accord he went unto you." The witnesses note Titus's ready and willing acceptance of the mission. Gill notes Titus accepted the exhortation to go and finish what he had begun; and not only so, but being more forward, of his own accord he went. Barnes notes he was eager to go, beyond the mere exhortation. The witnesses note Paul declaring that Titus accepted the exhortation, and being more forward, went of his own accord; the eager willingness of the messenger — that Titus not only consented when urged to undertake the journey, but was so zealous that he went of his own accord, his own eager love for the Corinthians outrunning the apostle's request, a model of glad and ready service; of his own accord he went. The witnesses note he accepted the exhortation. The witnesses note of his own accord he went unto you.

The witnesses note Titus's eager willingness — accepting the exhortation, yet so forward he went of his own accord; a model of glad, ready service.

So the witnesses make the verse Titus's eager acceptance of the mission. The lesson the expositors draw is the eager willingness of the messenger, his own love outrunning the apostle's request. The witnesses note the acceptance: "he accepted the exhortation" — Gill notes Titus accepted the exhortation to go; the acceptance; the exhortation; the consented; the to go; the agreed. The expositors observe the more forward: "but being more forward" — Titus was more forward (eager, zealous); the more forward; the eager; the zealous; the beyond; the willing. The witnesses press the own accord: "of his own accord he went unto you" — of his own accord he went to them; the own accord; the went; the unasked; the spontaneous; the eager. The expositors draw the lesson on the eager willingness: Titus not only consented when urged, but was so zealous he went of his own accord; the eager willingness; the consented; the zealous; the own accord; the willing. The witnesses observe the love outrunning the request: Titus's own eager love outran the apostle's request; the love; the outran; the request; the own eager; the willing. The expositors note the model of service: Titus is a model of glad and ready service; the model; the glad; the ready; the service; the example. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the he accepted the exhortation, the of his own accord he went unto you, and the eager willingness. The verse thus records Titus's eager acceptance of the mission — teaching the eager willingness of the messenger, his own love outrunning the apostle's request.
18:
"And we have sent with him the brother, whose praise is in the gospel throughout all the churches;" The witnesses note the sending of a renowned brother with Titus. Gill notes the identity of this brother is uncertain; some think it was Luke the evangelist, the apostle's companion. Barnes notes it has generally been supposed this anonymous brother was Luke. The witnesses note Paul declaring that they sent with Titus the brother whose praise in the gospel is throughout all the churches; the choosing of a man of proven reputation — that Paul joined to Titus a fellow-worker (unnamed, but widely thought to be Luke) whose faithful gospel labours had earned him honour in all the churches, so that the men entrusted with the collection were of unimpeachable standing; whose praise is in the gospel. The witnesses note the brother, whose praise is in the gospel. The witnesses note throughout all the churches.

The witnesses note the sending of a brother of proven reputation — his gospel labours honoured in all the churches — to accompany Titus with the collection.

So the witnesses make the verse the sending of a renowned brother. The lesson the expositors draw is the choosing of a man of proven reputation, of unimpeachable standing, to handle the collection. The witnesses note the brother: "we have sent with him the brother" — Paul sent a brother with Titus; the brother; the sent; the with Titus; the fellow-worker; the companion. The expositors observe the uncertain identity: Gill and Barnes note the brother is unnamed, widely thought to be Luke; the uncertain; the unnamed; the Luke; the supposed; the identity. The witnesses press the praise: "whose praise is in the gospel" — his praise is in the gospel (his gospel labours honoured); the praise; the in the gospel; the labours; the honoured; the reputation. The expositors draw the lesson on the proven reputation: the brother was of proven reputation — his faithful labours earning honour; the proven reputation; the faithful labours; the honour; the standing; the esteemed. The witnesses observe the all the churches: "throughout all the churches" — his praise was throughout all the churches; the all the churches; the widespread; the honoured; the everywhere; the reputation. The expositors note the unimpeachable standing: the men entrusted with the collection were of unimpeachable standing — to guard against suspicion; the unimpeachable; the standing; the entrusted; the collection; the guard. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the brother whose praise is in the gospel, the throughout all the churches, and the man of proven reputation. The verse thus records the sending of a renowned brother — teaching the choosing of a man of proven reputation, of unimpeachable standing, to handle the collection.
19:
"And not that only, but who was also chosen of the churches to travel with us with this grace, which is administered by us to the glory of the same Lord, and declaration of your ready mind:" The witnesses note that the brother was chosen by the churches to help carry the collection. Gill notes the brother was not only famous for preaching but was also appointed by the joint suffrages of the churches (by the lifting up of hands), chosen not by a few or a single church, but by several. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the brother was chosen by the churches to travel with them in this grace (the collection), administered to the glory of the Lord and the declaration of their ready mind; the open, accountable handling of the gift — that the bearer of the collection was formally elected by the churches themselves, so that the money was carried under public appointment, the whole administered for the Lord's glory and as a proof of the givers' willing heart; chosen of the churches. The witnesses note chosen of the churches to travel with us. The witnesses note to the glory of the same Lord.

The witnesses note the brother chosen by the churches to help carry the collection — the gift handled openly, under public appointment, for the Lord's glory.

So the witnesses make the verse the church-appointed bearer of the collection. The lesson the expositors draw is the open, accountable handling of the gift, the bearer formally elected by the churches. The witnesses note the choosing: "chosen of the churches to travel with us" — Gill notes the brother was appointed by the joint suffrages of the churches; the choosing; the chosen; the the churches; the suffrages; the appointed. The expositors observe the with this grace: "with this grace, which is administered by us" — to travel with them in this grace (the collection), administered by them; the with this grace; the the collection; the administered; the carried; the gift. The witnesses press the glory of the Lord: "to the glory of the same Lord" — the collection administered to the glory of the Lord; the glory; the the Lord; the for his glory; the administered; the aim. The expositors draw the lesson on the open handling: the bearer was formally elected by the churches — the money carried under public appointment; the open handling; the elected; the public appointment; the accountable; the money. The witnesses observe the declaration of ready mind: "and declaration of your ready mind" — and as a declaration (proof) of the givers' ready mind; the declaration; the ready mind; the proof; the willing; the givers. The expositors note the accountability: the open, accountable handling guards the gift and the apostle's name; the accountability; the open; the guards; the gift; the name. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the chosen of the churches to travel with us, the to the glory of the same Lord, and the church-appointed bearer. The verse thus records the church-appointed bearer of the collection — teaching the open, accountable handling of the gift, the bearer formally elected by the churches.
20:
"Avoiding this, that no man should blame us in this abundance which is administered by us:" The witnesses note Paul's care to avoid all blame in handling the money. Gill notes there is an allusion to mariners, who, sensing danger, steer another course to shun a rock; so the apostles, aware of censorious spirits, took care to prevent all suspicion of converting any part of the collection to their own use. The witnesses note Paul stating that they took these precautions, avoiding any occasion for blame in the large sum administered by them; the careful avoidance of suspicion — that Paul deliberately arranged for the collection to be handled by chosen, trusted men, so that no one could accuse him of dishonesty in managing so large a contribution, guarding his ministry against even the appearance of corruption; avoiding that no man should blame us. The witnesses note avoiding this, that no man should blame us. The witnesses note this abundance which is administered by us.

The witnesses note Paul's care to avoid all blame in handling the money — arranging trusted bearers so none could accuse him of dishonesty.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's care to avoid suspicion. The lesson the expositors draw is the careful avoidance of any occasion for blame in handling the large contribution. The witnesses note the avoiding: "avoiding this, that no man should blame us" — Gill notes Paul, aware of censorious spirits, took care to prevent suspicion; the avoiding; the no blame; the censorious; the prevent; the suspicion. The expositors observe the mariner image: Gill notes the allusion to mariners steering clear of a rock — Paul steering clear of blame; the mariner; the rock; the steering clear; the danger; the avoiding. The witnesses press the abundance: "this abundance which is administered by us" — the large sum (abundance) administered by them; the abundance; the large sum; the administered; the collection; the money. The expositors draw the lesson on the avoidance of suspicion: Paul deliberately arranged trusted men, so none could accuse him of dishonesty; the avoidance; the suspicion; the trusted men; the no accusation; the dishonesty. The witnesses observe the guarding of the ministry: Paul guards his ministry against even the appearance of corruption; the guarding; the ministry; the appearance; the corruption; the careful. The expositors note the prudence: Paul's prudence in money matters — open, accountable, above reproach; the prudence; the money; the open; the accountable; the reproach. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the avoiding this that no man should blame us, the this abundance which is administered by us, and the care to avoid suspicion. The verse thus records Paul's care to avoid suspicion — teaching the careful avoidance of any occasion for blame in handling the large contribution.
21:
"Providing for honest things, not only in the sight of the Lord, but also in the sight of men." The witnesses note the principle of integrity before both God and men. Gill notes "providing" means premeditating, forecasting beforehand in the mind, things that are good and of good report among men, as well as accounted good by God; for professors, especially ministers, should so behave as to keep a good conscience toward God and a good name among men. The witnesses note Paul declaring that they provide for honest things, not only in the Lord's sight but also in men's; the double standard of integrity — that Paul takes deliberate forethought to do what is right and honourable not only before God (who sees the heart) but also before men (who watch the conduct), so that his ministry is clear of reproach in both the divine and the human view, his honesty being open and visible; honest things in the sight of the Lord and of men. The witnesses note providing for honest things. The witnesses note in the sight of the Lord... also in the sight of men.

The witnesses note the principle of integrity before both God and men — doing what is honourable not only in God's sight but visibly before men.

So the witnesses make the verse integrity before God and men. The lesson the expositors draw is the double standard of integrity — doing right not only before God who sees the heart, but visibly before men. The witnesses note the providing: "providing for honest things" — Gill notes "providing" is forecasting beforehand things good and of good report; the providing; the forethought; the honest things; the good; the report. The expositors observe the sight of the Lord: "not only in the sight of the Lord" — honest before God, who sees the heart; the sight of the Lord; the before God; the the heart; the conscience; the divine. The witnesses press the sight of men: "but also in the sight of men" — but also before men, who watch the conduct; the sight of men; the before men; the watch; the conduct; the visible. The expositors draw the lesson on the double standard: Paul takes forethought to do right both before God and before men — honest in both views; the double standard; the both; the before God and men; the honest; the integrity. The witnesses observe the open honesty: Paul's honesty is open and visible — not only inward but evident to men; the open honesty; the visible; the inward and evident; the men; the integrity. The expositors note the good name: the minister should keep a good conscience toward God and a good name among men; the good name; the conscience; the toward God; the among men; the reputation. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the providing for honest things, the in the sight of the Lord and of men, and the integrity before both. The verse thus records integrity before God and men — teaching the double standard of integrity, doing right not only before God but visibly before men.
22:
"And we have sent with them our brother, whom we have oftentimes proved diligent in many things, but now much more diligent, upon the great confidence which I have in you." The witnesses note the sending of a third tried and diligent brother. Gill notes this is a third person sent about the business; the apostle conformed to the custom (or it agrees with the Jewish canon requiring three persons for the distribution of alms). The witnesses note Paul declaring that they sent with them a brother, often proved diligent, now more diligent through his great confidence in the Corinthians; the third trusted delegate — that Paul added a third man, one whose diligence he had tested many times and who was now all the more zealous because of the confidence Paul had expressed in the Corinthians, completing a trio of proven men to carry the gift with full accountability; our brother... proved diligent. The witnesses note our brother, whom we have oftentimes proved diligent. The witnesses note now much more diligent.

The witnesses note the sending of a third proven, diligent brother — now the more zealous through confidence in the Corinthians; a trio of trusted men.

So the witnesses make the verse the sending of a third tried brother. The lesson the expositors draw is the completing of a trio of proven men, this third tested often and now the more diligent. The witnesses note the third brother: "we have sent with them our brother" — Gill notes this is a third person sent (a trio, per the Jewish custom for alms); the third brother; the sent; the trio; the custom; the three. The expositors observe the proven diligence: "whom we have oftentimes proved diligent in many things" — a brother often proved diligent in many things; the proven diligence; the oftentimes; the diligent; the tested; the many things. The witnesses press the more diligent: "but now much more diligent" — now much more diligent; the more diligent; the now; the increased; the zealous; the eager. The expositors draw the lesson on the great confidence: "upon the great confidence which I have in you" — the more diligent through Paul's great confidence in the Corinthians; the great confidence; the in you; the the more; the zealous; the trust. The witnesses observe the trio of proven men: Paul completed a trio of proven, trusted men to carry the gift; the trio; the proven men; the trusted; the carry the gift; the three. The expositors note the full accountability: the trio ensured full accountability for the collection; the accountability; the trio; the collection; the full; the trusted. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the our brother whom we have oftentimes proved diligent, the now much more diligent, and the third tried brother. The verse thus records the sending of a third tried brother — teaching the completing of a trio of proven men, this third tested often and now the more diligent.
23:
"Whether any do enquire of Titus, he is my partner and fellowhelper concerning you: or our brethren be enquired of, they are the messengers of the churches, and the glory of Christ." The witnesses note Paul's commendation of the three messengers. Gill notes the apostle here sums up the characters of each of these persons — as for Titus, his partner and fellow-helper. The witnesses note Paul commending the delegates — Titus as his partner and fellow-helper toward them, and the brethren as messengers of the churches and the glory of Christ; the high honour given to faithful servants — that Paul vouches for all three, naming Titus his partner in labour and the others the official representatives ("apostles") of the churches and an honour to Christ himself, so that the Corinthians should receive them as men of the highest worth; messengers of the churches, the glory of Christ. The witnesses note he is my partner and fellowhelper. The witnesses note the messengers of the churches, and the glory of Christ.

The witnesses note Paul commending the three messengers — Titus his partner, the brethren messengers of the churches and the glory of Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the commendation of the messengers. The lesson the expositors draw is the high honour given to faithful servants, Paul vouching for all three. The witnesses note the partner: "whether any do enquire of Titus, he is my partner and fellowhelper concerning you" — Gill notes Titus is named Paul's partner and fellow-helper; the partner; the fellow-helper; the Titus; the concerning you; the labour. The expositors observe the messengers: "or our brethren be enquired of, they are the messengers of the churches" — the brethren are the messengers (literally "apostles") of the churches; the messengers; the the churches; the representatives; the apostles; the official. The witnesses press the glory of Christ: "and the glory of Christ" — and the glory of Christ (an honour to him); the glory of Christ; the honour; the to him; the reflect; the Christ. The expositors draw the lesson on the high honour: Paul gives high honour to these faithful servants — vouching for all three; the high honour; the faithful servants; the vouching; the all three; the worth. The witnesses observe the official representatives: the brethren are the official representatives of the churches — chosen, sent, trusted; the official; the representatives; the churches; the chosen; the sent. The expositors note the men of worth: the Corinthians should receive them as men of the highest worth; the men of worth; the receive; the highest; the worth; the honour. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the he is my partner and fellowhelper, the the messengers of the churches and the glory of Christ, and the commendation. The verse thus records the commendation of the messengers — teaching the high honour given to faithful servants, Paul vouching for all three.
24:
"Wherefore shew ye to them, and before the churches, the proof of your love, and of our boasting on your behalf." The witnesses note the closing call to demonstrate their love and vindicate Paul's boast. Gill notes this is the conclusion — since these messengers were persons of such note and worth, the Corinthians ought to receive them with great marks of respect and affection. The witnesses note Paul exhorting them to show, before the churches, the proof of their love and of his boasting on their behalf; the call to make their love evident — that the Corinthians, by their generous giving and their warm reception of the delegates, are to give open and public proof both of the genuineness of their love and of the truth of Paul's confident boasting about them, so that their conduct vindicates his praise before the watching churches; the proof of your love. The witnesses note shew ye... the proof of your love. The witnesses note our boasting on your behalf.

The witnesses note the closing call — show the proof of your love before the churches, vindicating Paul's boast by generous giving and warm reception.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to demonstrate love and vindicate the boast. The lesson the expositors draw is that the Corinthians, by their giving and reception of the delegates, should give open proof of their love and the truth of Paul's boast. The witnesses note the showing: "shew ye to them, and before the churches" — Gill notes the Corinthians ought to receive the messengers with respect and affection, before the churches; the showing; the to them; the before the churches; the openly; the public. The expositors observe the proof of love: "the proof of your love" — the proof (demonstration) of their love; the proof; the love; the demonstration; the evident; the genuine. The witnesses press the boasting: "and of our boasting on your behalf" — and the proof of Paul's boasting on their behalf; the boasting; the on their behalf; the praise; the confidence; the boast. The expositors draw the lesson on making love evident: the Corinthians are to give open and public proof of the genuineness of their love; the making evident; the open proof; the public; the genuineness; the love. The witnesses observe the vindicating the boast: their conduct (giving, reception) vindicates Paul's confident boast before the churches; the vindicating; the conduct; the the boast; the before the churches; the proof. The expositors note the watching churches: the proof is given before the watching churches — a public vindication; the watching churches; the public; the vindication; the before; the proof. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the shew ye the proof of your love, the our boasting on your behalf, and the call to demonstrate love. The verse thus records the call to demonstrate love and vindicate the boast — teaching that the Corinthians should give open proof of their love and the truth of Paul's boast before the churches.
